facade_colour: brown-red brick, somewhat dark and muted

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The properties retain original building features including a cross gabled roofline, mirrored facades, double-height bay windows and fenestration pattern. 153 Denison Avenue has also retained its brick cladding and wooden decorative wooden gable brackets. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: Local | Residential | Transition to Bellevue Park

photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Facade heavily modified with added balconies and metal railings at multiple levels, original character compromised
photo_observations.window_details: Mix of replacement windows, some with white frames, horizontal sliding and casement types
photo_observations.door_details: Ground-level entry, metal security gate/railing at entrance
photo_observations.cornice_details: Simple dark eave, partially hidden by additions
photo_observations.bay_window_details: Original double-height bay visible but partially obscured by balcony additions
photo_observations.porch_details: Concrete stoop with metal railings, stairs to upper balcony
photo_observations.decorative_details: Original decorative elements mostly hidden by additions
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Major alterations: projecting metal balconies added at first and second floor levels, metal railings throughout, enclosed sections, replacement windows
photo_observations.notes: Heavily modified bay-and-gable semi-detached; balcony additions significantly alter the original facade character noted in HCD statement

facade_description: 151 Denison Ave presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, bay window, original windows, cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The properties retain original building features including a cross gabled roofline, mirrored facades, double-height bay windows and fenestration pattern. 153 Denison Avenue has also retained its brick cladding and wooden decorative wooden gable brackets. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
heritage_summary: 153 Denison Avenue has also retained its brick cladding and wooden decorative wooden gable brackets. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.
